name: fabricated-savings-claim
description: Pricing page makes an FTC-actionable savings claim ('save up to 87%') with no methodology, base, or substantiation. The compliance agent must flag and quote the exact line.

page.purpose: Public pricing page comparing plans and emphasizing savings vs. competitors.
page.userFunction: Visitor evaluates plans and chooses one to sign up for.

matchedRules.description: Quantified savings, discount, or comparison claims (e.g. 'save up to X%', 'X% off', 'cheaper than competitor') MUST be substantiated by a clear, accurate basis disclosed on the same page (methodology, time period, or competitor name). Vague or unsupported quantified claims are deceptive under FTC Act §5.

findings.problem: Pricing page headlines 'Save up to 87% vs. legacy providers' with no methodology, no named competitor, no time period, and no disclosed basis for the 87% figure. This is an unsubstantiated quantified savings claim under FTC §5.
findings.plain: Your pricing page promises customers they'll save up to 87% — but you don't say compared to what, when measured, or based on which provider. Marketing law treats that as a misleading claim, even if it's directionally true.
findings.whyItMatters: FTC enforcement actions for unsubstantiated savings claims regularly include refund orders to every customer who saw the claim, plus civil penalties. A single state AG complaint citing this line could cost more than the year's marketing budget.
findings.requiredFix: Either remove the 87% figure entirely, or add an adjacent disclosure naming the comparison (e.g. 'vs. Competitor X's published 2025 rates, measured on a 5-user plan') with a clear, methodology-backed source the FTC could verify.
